content.content:
# Claude Code Status Line Configuration

Based on the documentation, Claude Code allows you to **customize your status bar to monitor context window usage, costs, and git status**.

## Documentation Reference

The relevant guide is: **[Customize your status line](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/statusline.md)**

This document covers how to configure a custom status bar in Claude Code with details on available JSON fields and configuration options.

## Key Capabilities

You can monitor:
- **Context window usage** - Track how much of your token budget is consumed
- **Costs** - Monitor API costs in real-time
- **Git status** - Display repository state information

## How to Access

To learn about the specific JSON fields and configuration syntax:
1. Visit the [Customize your status line](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/statusline.md) documentation
2. Review the configuration schema for available fields
3. Place your status line configuration in your `.claude` directory or global settings

This feature is available across Claude Code platforms including the CLI, Desktop app, and VS Code extension.

input.prompt: JSON input fields available to statusLine command script, context window, token usage, stdin format

It receives JSON session data on stdin and displays whatever your script prints, giving you a persistent, at-a-glance view of context usage, costs, git status, or anything else you want to track. Status lines are useful when you: * Want to monitor context window usage as you work * Need to track session costs * Work across multiple sessions and need to distinguish them * Want git branch and status always visible Here's an example of a [multi-line status line](#display-multiple-lines) that displays git info on the first line and a color-coded context bar on the second. This page walks through [setting up a basic status line](#set-up-a-status-line), explains [how the data flows](#how-status-lines-work) from Claude Code to your script, lists [all the fields you can display](#available-data), and provides [ready-to-use examples](#examples) for common patterns like git status, cost tracking, and progress bars. ## Set up a status line ...

content.content: 1 > ## Documentation Index 2 > Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://code.claude.com/docs/llms.txt 3 > Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further. 4 5 # Customize your status line 6 7 > Configure a custom status bar to monitor context window usage, costs, and git status in Claude Code 8 9 The status line is a customizable bar at the bottom of Claude Code that runs any shell script you configure. It receives JSON session data on stdin and displays whatever your script prints, giving you a persistent, at-a-glance view of context usage, costs, git status, or anything else you want to track. 10 11 Status lines are useful when you: 12 13 * Want to monitor context window usage as you work 14 * Need to track session costs 15 * Work across multiple sessions and need to distinguish them 16 * Want git branch and status always visible 17 18 Here's an example of a [multi-line status line](#display-multiple-lines) that displays git info on the first line and a color-coded context bar on the second. 19 20 21 22 23 24 This page walks through [setting up a basic status line](#set-up-a-status-line), explains [how the data flows](#how-status-lines-work) from Claude Code to your script, lists [all the fields you can display](#available-data), and provides [ready-to-use examples](#examples) for common patterns like git status, cost tracking, and progress bars. 25 26 ## Set up a status line 27 28 Use the [`/statusline` command](#use-the-statusline-command) to have Claude Code generate a script for you, or [manually create a script](#manually-configure-a-status-line) and add it to your settings. 29 30 ### Use the /statusline command 31 32 The `/statusline` command accepts natural language instructions describing what you want displayed. Claude Code generates a script file in `~/.claude/` and updates your settings automatically: 33 34 ```text theme={null} 35 /statusline show model name and context percentage with a progress bar 36 ``` 37 38 ### Manually configure a status line 39 40 Add a `statusLine` field to your user settings (`~/.claude/settings.json`, where `~` is your home directory) or [project settings](/en/settings#settings-files). Set `type` to `"command"` and point `command` to a script path or an inline shell command. For a full walkthrough of creating a script, see [Build a status line step by step](#build-a-status-line-step-by-step). 41 42 ```json theme={null} 43 { 44 "statusLine": { 45 "type": "command", 46 "command": "~/.claude/statusline.sh", 47 "padding": 2 48 } 49 } 50 ``` 51 52 The `command` field runs in a shell, so you can also use inline commands instead of a script file. This example uses `jq` to parse the JSON input and display the model name and context percentage: 53 54 ```json theme={null} 55 { 56 "statusLine": { 57 "type": "command", 58 "command": "jq -r '\"[\\(.model.display_name)] \\(.context_window.used_percentage // 0)% context\"'" 59 } 60 } 61 ``` 62 63 The optional `padding` field adds extra horizontal spacing (in characters) to the status line content. Defaults to `0`. This padding is in addition to the interface's built-in spacing, so it controls relative indentation rather than absolute distance from the terminal edge. 64 65 The optional `refreshInterval` field re-runs your command every N seconds in addition to the [event-driven updates](#how-status-lines-work). The minimum is `1`. Set this when your status line shows time-based data such as a clock, or when background subagents change git state while the main session is idle. Leave it unset to run only on events. 66 67 ### Disable the status line 68 69 Run `/statusline` and ask it to remove or clear your status line (e.g., `/statusline delete`, `/statusline clear`, `/statusline remove it`). You can also manually delete the `statusLine` field from your settings.json. 70 71 ## Build a status line step by step 72 73 This walkthrough shows what's happening under the hood by manually creating a status line that displays the current model, working directory, and context window usage percentage. 74 75 Running [`/statusline`](#use-the-statusline-command) with a description of what you want configures all of this for you automatically. 76 77 These examples use Bash scripts, which work on macOS and Linux. On Windows, see [Windows configuration](#windows-configuration) for PowerShell and Git Bash examples. 78 79 80 81 82 83 84 85 Claude Code sends JSON data to your script via stdin. This script uses [`jq`](https://jqlang.github.io/jq/), a command-line JSON parser you may need to install, to extract the model name, directory, and context percentage, then prints a formatted line. 86 87 Save this to `~/.claude/statusline.sh` (where `~` is your home directory, such as `/Users/username` on macOS or `/home/username` on Linux): 88 89 ```bash theme={null} 90 #!/bin/bash 91 # Read JSON data that Claude Code sends to stdin 92 input=$(cat) 93 94 # Extract fields using jq 95 MODEL=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name') 96 DIR=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.workspace.current_dir') 97 # The "// 0" provides a fallback if the field is null 98 PCT=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.context_window.used_percentage // 0' | cut -d. -f1) 99 100 # Output the status line - ${DIR##*/} extracts just the folder name 101 echo "[$MODEL] 📁 ${DIR##*/} | ${PCT}% context" 102 ``` 103 104 105 106 Mark the script as executable so your shell can run it: 107 108 ```bash theme={null} 109 chmod +x ~/.claude/statusline.sh 110 ``` 111 112 113 114 Tell Claude Code to run your script as the status line. Add this configuration to `~/.claude/settings.json`, which sets `type` to `"command"` (meaning "run this shell command") and points `command` to your script: 115 116 ```json theme={null} 117 { 118 "statusLine": { 119 "type": "command", 120 "command": "~/.claude/statusline.sh" 121 } 122 } 123 ``` 124 125 Your status line appears at the bottom of the interface. Settings reload automatically, but changes won't appear until your next interaction with Claude Code. 126 127 128 129 ## How status lines work 130 131 Claude Code runs your script and pipes [JSON session data](#available-data) to it via stdin. Your script reads the JSON, extracts what it needs, and prints text to stdout. Claude Code displays whatever your script prints. 132 133 **When it updates** 134 135 Your script runs after each new assistant message, when the permission mode changes, or when vim mode toggles. Updates are debounced at 300ms, meaning rapid changes batch together and your script runs once things settle. If a new update triggers while your script is still running, the in-flight execution is cancelled. If you edit your script, the changes won't appear until your next interaction with Claude Code triggers an update. 136 137 These triggers can go quiet when the main session is idle, for example while a coordinator waits on background subagents. To keep time-based or externally-sourced segments current during idle periods, set [`refreshInterval`](#manually-configure-a-status-line) to also re-run the command on a fixed timer. 138 139 **What your script can output** 140 141 * **Multiple lines**: each `echo` or `print` statement displays as a separate row. See the [multi-line example](#display-multiple-lines). 142 * **Colors**: use [ANSI escape codes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#Colors) like `\033[32m` for green (terminal must support them). See the [git status example](#git-status-with-colors). 143 * **Links**: use [OSC 8 escape sequences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#OSC) to make text clickable (Cmd+click on macOS, Ctrl+click on Windows/Linux). Requires a terminal that supports hyperlinks like iTerm2, Kitty, or WezTerm. See the [clickable links example](#clickable-links). 144 145 The status line runs locally and does not consume API tokens. It temporarily hides during certain UI interactions, including autocomplete suggestions, the help menu, and permission prompts. 146 147 ## Available data 148 149 Claude Code sends the following JSON fields to your script via stdin: 150 151 | Field | Description | 152 | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | 153 | `model.id`, `model.display_name` | Current model identifier and display name | 154 | `cwd`, `workspace.current_dir` | Current working directory. Both fields contain the same value; `workspace.current_dir` is preferred for consistency with `workspace.project_dir`. | 155 | `workspace.project_dir` | Directory where Claude Code was launched, which may differ from `cwd` if the working directory changes during a session | 156 | `workspace.added_dirs` | Additional directories added via `/add-dir` or `--add-dir`. Empty array if none have been added | 157 | `workspace.git_worktree` | Git worktree name when the current directory is inside a linked worktree created with `git worktree add`. Absent in the main working tree. Populated for any git worktree, unlike `worktree.*` which applies only to `--worktree` sessions | 158 | `cost.total_cost_usd` | Estimated session cost in USD, computed client-side. May differ from your actual bill | 159 | `cost.total_duration_ms` | Total wall-clock time since the session started, in milliseconds | 160 | `cost.total_api_duration_ms` | Total time spent waiting for API responses in milliseconds | 161 | `cost.total_lines_added`, `cost.total_lines_removed` | Lines of code changed | 162 | `context_window.total_input_tokens`, `context_window.total_output_tokens` | Cumulative token counts across the session | 163 | `context_window.context_window_size` | Maximum context window size in tokens. 200000 by default, or 1000000 for models with extended context. | 164 | `context_window.used_percentage` | Pre-calculated percentage of context window used | 165 | `context_window.remaining_percentage` | Pre-calculated percentage of context window remaining | 166 | `context_window.current_usage` | Token counts from the last API call, described in [context window fields](#context-window-fields) | 167 | `exceeds_200k_tokens` | Whether the total token count (input, cache, and output tokens combined) from the most recent API response exceeds 200k. This is a fixed threshold regardless of actual context window size. | 168 | `rate_limits.five_hour.used_percentage`, `rate_limits.seven_day.used_percentage` | Percentage of the 5-hour or 7-day rate limit consumed, from 0 to 100 | 169 | `rate_limits.five_hour.resets_at`, `rate_limits.seven_day.resets_at` | Unix epoch seconds when the 5-hour or 7-day rate limit window resets | 170 | `session_id` | Unique session identifier | 171 | `session_name` | Custom session name set with the `--name` flag or `/rename`. Absent if no custom name has been set | 172 | `transcript_path` | Path to conversation transcript file | 173 | `version` | Claude Code version | 174 | `output_style.name` | Name of the current output style | 175 | `vim.mode` | Current vim mode (`NORMAL` or `INSERT`) when [vim mode](/en/interactive-mode#vim-editor-mode) is enabled | 176 | `agent.name` | Agent name when running with the `--agent` flag or agent settings configured | 177 | `worktree.name` | Name of the active worktree. Present only during `--worktree` sessions | 178 | `worktree.path` | Absolute path to the worktree directory | 179 | `worktree.branch` | Git branch name for the worktree (for example, `"worktree-my-feature"`). Absent for hook-based worktrees | 180 | `worktree.original_cwd` | The directory Claude was in before entering the worktree | 181 | `worktree.original_branch` | Git branch checked out before entering the worktree. Absent for hook-based worktrees | 182 183 184 Your status line command receives this JSON structure via stdin: 185 186 ```json theme={null} 187 { 188 "cwd": "/current/working/directory", 189 "session_id": "abc123...", 190 "session_name": "my-session", 191 "transcript_path": "/path/to/transcript.jsonl", 192 "model": { 193 "id": "claude-opus-4-7", 194 "display_name": "Opus" 195 }, 196 "workspace": { 197 "current_dir": "/current/working/directory", 198 "project_dir": "/original/project/directory", 199 "added_dirs": [], 200 "git_worktree": "feature-xyz" 201 }, 202 "version": "2.1.90", 203 "output_style": { 204 "name": "default" 205 }, 206 "cost": { 207 "total_cost_usd": 0.01234, 208 "total_duration_ms": 45000, 209 "total_api_duration_ms": 2300, 210 "total_lines_added": 156, 211 "total_lines_removed": 23 212 }, 213 "context_window": { 214 "total_input_tokens": 15234, 215 "total_output_tokens": 4521, 216 "context_window_size": 200000, 217 "used_percentage": 8, 218 "remaining_percentage": 92, 219 "current_usage": { 220 "input_tokens": 8500, 221 "output_tokens": 1200, 222 "cache_creation_input_tokens": 5000, 223 "cache_read_input_tokens": 2000 224 } 225 }, 226 "exceeds_200k_tokens": false, 227 "rate_limits": { 228 "five_hour": { 229 "used_percentage": 23.5, 230 "resets_at": 1738425600 231 }, 232 "seven_day": { 233 "used_percentage": 41.2, 234 "resets_at": 1738857600 235 } 236 }, 237 "vim": { 238 "mode": "NORMAL" 239 }, 240 "agent": { 241 "name": "security-reviewer" 242 }, 243 "worktree": { 244 "name": "my-feature", 245 "path": "/path/to/.claude/worktrees/my-feature", 246 "branch": "worktree-my-feature", 247 "original_cwd": "/path/to/project", 248 "original_branch": "main" 249 } 250 } 251 ``` 252 253 **Fields that may be absent** (not present in JSON): 254 255 * `session_name`: appears only when a custom name has been set with `--name` or `/rename` 256 * `workspace.git_worktree`: appears only when the current directory is inside a linked git worktree 257 * `vim`: appears only when vim mode is enabled 258 * `agent`: appears only when running with the `--agent` flag or agent settings configured 259 * `worktree`: appears only during `--worktree` sessions. When present, `branch` and `original_branch` may also be absent for hook-based worktrees 260 * `rate_limits`: appears only for Claude.ai subscribers (Pro/Max) after the first API response in the session. Each window (`five_hour`, `seven_day`) may be independently absent. Use `jq -r '.rate_limits.five_hour.used_percentage // empty'` to handle absence gracefully. 261 262 **Fields that may be `null`**: 263 264 * `context_window.current_usage`: `null` before the first API call in a session 265 * `context_window.used_percentage`, `context_window.remaining_percentage`: may be `null` early in the session 266 267 Handle missing fields with conditional access and null values with fallback defaults in your scripts. 268 269 270 ### Context window fields 271 272 The `context_window` object provides two ways to track context usage: 273 274 * **Cumulative totals** (`total_input_tokens`, `total_output_tokens`): sum of all tokens across the entire session, useful for tracking total consumption 275 * **Current usage** (`current_usage`): token counts from the most recent API call, use this for accurate context percentage since it reflects the actual context state 276 277 The `current_usage` object contains: 278 279 * `input_tokens`: input tokens in current context 280 * `output_tokens`: output tokens generated 281 * `cache_creation_input_tokens`: tokens written to cache 282 * `cache_read_input_tokens`: tokens read from cache 283 284 The `used_percentage` field is calculated from input tokens only: `input_tokens + cache_creation_input_tokens + cache_read_input_tokens`. It does not include `output_tokens`. 285 286 If you calculate context percentage manually from `current_usage`, use the same input-only formula to match `used_percentage`. 287 288 The `current_usage` object is `null` before the first API call in a session. 289 290 ## Examples 291 292 These examples show common status line patterns. To use any example: 293 294 1. Save the script to a file like `~/.claude/statusline.sh` (or `.py`/`.js`) 295 2. Make it executable: `chmod +x ~/.claude/statusline.sh` 296 3. Add the path to your [settings](#manually-configure-a-status-line) 297 298 The Bash examples use [`jq`](https://jqlang.github.io/jq/) to parse JSON. Python and Node.js have built-in JSON parsing. 299 300 ### Context window usage 301 302 Display the current model and context window usage with a visual progress bar. Each script reads JSON from stdin, extracts the `used_percentage` field, and builds a 10-character bar where filled blocks (▓) represent usage: 303 304 305 306 307 308 309 ```bash Bash theme={null} 310 #!/bin/bash 311 # Read all of stdin into a variable 312 input=$(cat) 313 314 # Extract fields with jq, "// 0" provides fallback for null 315 MODEL=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name') 316 PCT=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.context_window.used_percentage // 0' | cut -d. -f1) 317 318 # Build progress bar: printf -v creates a run of spaces, then 319 # ${var// /▓} replaces each space with a block character 320 BAR_WIDTH=10 321 FILLED=$((PCT * BAR_WIDTH / 100)) 322 EMPTY=$((BAR_WIDTH - FILLED)) 323 BAR="" 324 [ "$FILLED" -gt 0 ] && printf -v FILL "%${FILLED}s" && BAR="${FILL// /▓}" 325 [ "$EMPTY" -gt 0 ] && printf -v PAD "%${EMPTY}s" && BAR="${BAR}${PAD// /░}" 326 327 echo "[$MODEL] $BAR $PCT%" 328 ``` 329 330 ```python Python theme={null} 331 #!/usr/bin/env python3 332 import json, sys 333 334 # json.load reads and parses stdin in one step 335 data = json.load(sys.stdin) 336 model = data['model']['display_name'] 337 # "or 0" handles null values 338 pct = int(data.get('context_window', {}).get('used_percentage', 0) or 0) 339 340 # String multiplication builds the bar 341 filled = pct * 10 // 100 342 bar = '▓' * filled + '░' * (10 - filled) 343 344 print(f"[{model}] {bar} {pct}%") 345 ``` 346 347 ```javascript Node.js theme={null} 348 #!/usr/bin/env node 349 // Node.js reads stdin asynchronously with events 350 let input = ''; 351 process.stdin.on('data', chunk => input += chunk); 352 process.stdin.on('end', () => { 353 const data = JSON.parse(input); 354 const model = data.model.display_name; 355 // Optional chaining (?.) safely handles null fields 356 const pct = Math.floor(data.context_window?.used_percentage || 0); 357 358 // String.repeat() builds the bar 359 const filled = Math.floor(pct * 10 / 100); 360 const bar = '▓'.repeat(filled) + '░'.repeat(10 - filled); 361 362 console.log(`[${model}] ${bar} ${pct}%`); 363 }); 364 ``` 365 366 367 ### Git status with colors 368 369 Show git branch with color-coded indicators for staged and modified files. This script uses [ANSI escape codes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#Colors) for terminal colors: `\033[32m` is green, `\033[33m` is yellow, and `\033[0m` resets to default. 370 371 372 373 374 375 Each script checks if the current directory is a git repository, counts staged and modified files, and displays color-coded indicators: 376 377 378 ```bash Bash theme={null} 379 #!/bin/bash 380 input=$(cat) 381 382 MODEL=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name') 383 DIR=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.workspace.current_dir') 384 385 GREEN='\033[32m' 386 YELLOW='\033[33m' 387 RESET='\033[0m' 388 389 if git rev-parse --git-dir > /dev/null 2>&1; then 390 BRANCH=$(git branch --show-current 2>/dev/null) 391 STAGED=$(git diff --cached --numstat 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ') 392 MODIFIED=$(git diff --numstat 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ') 393 394 GIT_STATUS="" 395 [ "$STAGED" -gt 0 ] && GIT_STATUS="${GREEN}+${STAGED}${RESET}" 396 [ "$MODIFIED" -gt 0 ] && GIT_STATUS="${GIT_STATUS}${YELLOW}~${MODIFIED}${RESET}" 397 398 echo -e "[$MODEL] 📁 ${DIR##*/} | 🌿 $BRANCH $GIT_STATUS" 399 else 400 echo "[$MODEL] 📁 ${DIR##*/}" 401 fi 402 ``` 403 404 ```python Python theme={null} 405 #!/usr/bin/env python3 406 import json, sys, subprocess, os 407 408 data = json.load(sys.stdin) 409 model = data['model']['display_name'] 410 directory = os.path.basename(data['workspace']['current_dir']) 411 412 GREEN, YELLOW, RESET = '\033[32m', '\033[33m', '\033[0m' 413 414 try: 415 subprocess.check_output(['git', 'rev-parse', '--git-dir'], stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL) 416 branch = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'branch', '--show-current'], text=True).strip() 417 staged_output = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'diff', '--cached', '--numstat'], text=True).strip() 418 modified_output = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'diff', '--numstat'], text=True).strip() 419 staged = len(staged_output.split('\n')) if staged_output else 0 420 modified = len(modified_output.split('\n')) if modified_output else 0 421 422 git_status = f"{GREEN}+{staged}{RESET}" if staged else "" 423 git_status += f"{YELLOW}~{modified}{RESET}" if modified else "" 424 425 print(f"[{model}] 📁 {directory} | 🌿 {branch} {git_status}") 426 except: 427 print(f"[{model}] 📁 {directory}") 428 ``` 429 430 ```javascript Node.js theme={null} 431 #!/usr/bin/env node 432 const { execSync } = require('child_process'); 433 const path = require('path'); 434 435 let input = ''; 436 process.stdin.on('data', chunk => input += chunk); 437 process.stdin.on('end', () => { 438 const data = JSON.parse(input); 439 const model = data.model.display_name; 440 const dir = path.basename(data.workspace.current_dir); 441 442 const GREEN = '\x1b[32m', YELLOW = '\x1b[33m', RESET = '\x1b[0m'; 443 444 try { 445 execSync('git rev-parse --git-dir', { stdio: 'ignore' }); 446 const branch = execSync('git branch --show-current', { encoding: 'utf8' }).trim(); 447 const staged = execSync('git diff --cached --numstat', { encoding: 'utf8' }).trim().split('\n').filter(Boolean).length; 448 const modified = execSync('git diff --numstat', { encoding: 'utf8' }).trim().split('\n').filter(Boolean).length; 449 450 let gitStatus = staged ? `${GREEN}+${staged}${RESET}` : ''; 451 gitStatus += modified ? `${YELLOW}~${modified}${RESET}` : ''; 452 453 console.log(`[${model}] 📁 ${dir} | 🌿 ${branch} ${gitStatus}`); 454 } catch { 455 console.log(`[${model}] 📁 ${dir}`); 456 } 457 }); 458 ``` 459 460 461 ### Cost and duration tracking 462 463 Track your session's API costs and elapsed time. The `cost.total_cost_usd` field accumulates the estimated cost of all API calls in the current session. The `cost.total_duration_ms` field measures total elapsed time since the session started, while `cost.total_api_duration_ms` tracks only the time spent waiting for API responses. 464 465 Each script formats cost as currency and converts milliseconds to minutes and seconds: 466 467 468 469 470 471 472 ```bash Bash theme={null} 473 #!/bin/bash 474 input=$(cat) 475 476 MODEL=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name') 477 COST=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.cost.total_cost_usd // 0') 478 DURATION_MS=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.cost.total_duration_ms // 0') 479 480 COST_FMT=$(printf '$%.2f' "$COST") 481 DURATION_SEC=$((DURATION_MS / 1000)) 482 MINS=$((DURATION_SEC / 60)) 483 SECS=$((DURATION_SEC % 60)) 484 485 echo "[$MODEL] 💰 $COST_FMT | ⏱️ ${MINS}m ${SECS}s" 486 ``` 487 488 ```python Python theme={null} 489 #!/usr/bin/env python3 490 import json, sys 491 492 data = json.load(sys.stdin) 493 model = data['model']['display_name'] 494 cost = data.get('cost', {}).get('total_cost_usd', 0) or 0 495 duration_ms = data.get('cost', {}).get('total_duration_ms', 0) or 0 496 497 duration_sec = duration_ms // 1000 498 mins, secs = duration_sec // 60, duration_sec % 60 499 500 print(f"[{model}] 💰 ${cost:.2f} | ⏱️ {mins}m {secs}s") 501 ``` 502 503 ```javascript Node.js theme={null} 504 #!/usr/bin/env node 505 let input = ''; 506 process.stdin.on('data', chunk => input += chunk); 507 process.stdin.on('end', () => { 508 const data = JSON.parse(input); 509 const model = data.model.display_name; 510 const cost = data.cost?.total_cost_usd || 0; 511 const durationMs = data.cost?.total_duration_ms || 0; 512 513 const durationSec = Math.floor(durationMs / 1000); 514 const mins = Math.floor(durationSec / 60); 515 const secs = durationSec % 60; 516 517 console.log(`[${model}] 💰 $${cost.toFixed(2)} | ⏱️ ${mins}m ${secs}s`); 518 }); 519 ``` 520 521 522 ### Display multiple lines 523 524 Your script can output multiple lines to create a richer display. Each `echo` statement produces a separate row in the status area. 525 526 527 528 529 530 This example combines several techniques: threshold-based colors (green under 70%, yellow 70-89%, red 90%+), a progress bar, and git branch info. Each `print` or `echo` statement creates a separate row: 531 532 533 ```bash Bash theme={null} 534 #!/bin/bash 535 input=$(cat) 536 537 MODEL=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name') 538 DIR=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.workspace.current_dir') 539 COST=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.cost.total_cost_usd // 0') 540 PCT=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.context_window.used_percentage // 0' | cut -d. -f1) 541 DURATION_MS=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.cost.total_duration_ms // 0') 542 543 CYAN='\033[36m'; GREEN='\033[32m'; YELLOW='\033[33m'; RED='\033[31m'; RESET='\033[0m' 544 545 # Pick bar color based on context usage 546 if [ "$PCT" -ge 90 ]; then BAR_COLOR="$RED" 547 elif [ "$PCT" -ge 70 ]; then BAR_COLOR="$YELLOW" 548 else BAR_COLOR="$GREEN"; fi 549 550 FILLED=$((PCT / 10)); EMPTY=$((10 - FILLED)) 551 printf -v FILL "%${FILLED}s"; printf -v PAD "%${EMPTY}s" 552 BAR="${FILL// /█}${PAD// /░}" 553 554 MINS=$((DURATION_MS / 60000)); SECS=$(((DURATION_MS % 60000) / 1000)) 555 556 BRANCH="" 557 git rev-parse --git-dir > /dev/null 2>&1 && BRANCH=" | 🌿 $(git branch --show-current 2>/dev/null)" 558 559 echo -e "${CYAN}[$MODEL]${RESET} 📁 ${DIR##*/}$BRANCH" 560 COST_FMT=$(printf '$%.2f' "$COST") 561 echo -e "${BAR_COLOR}${BAR}${RESET} ${PCT}% | ${YELLOW}${COST_FMT}${RESET} | ⏱️ ${MINS}m ${SECS}s" 562 ``` 563 564 ```python Python theme={null} 565 #!/usr/bin/env python3 566 import json, sys, subprocess, os 567 568 data = json.load(sys.stdin) 569 model = data['model']['display_name'] 570 directory = os.path.basename(data['workspace']['current_dir']) 571 cost = data.get('cost', {}).get('total_cost_usd', 0) or 0 572 pct = int(data.get('context_window', {}).get('used_percentage', 0) or 0) 573 duration_ms = data.get('cost', {}).get('total_duration_ms', 0) or 0 574 575 CYAN, GREEN, YELLOW, RED, RESET = '\033[36m', '\033[32m', '\033[33m', '\033[31m', '\033[0m' 576 577 bar_color = RED if pct >= 90 else YELLOW if pct >= 70 else GREEN 578 filled = pct // 10 579 bar = '█' * filled + '░' * (10 - filled) 580 581 mins, secs = duration_ms // 60000, (duration_ms % 60000) // 1000 582 583 try: 584 branch = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'branch', '--show-current'], text=True, stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).strip() 585 branch = f" | 🌿 {branch}" if branch else "" 586 except: 587 branch = "" 588 589 print(f"{CYAN}[{model}]{RESET} 📁 {directory}{branch}") 590 print(f"{bar_color}{bar}{RESET} {pct}% | {YELLOW}${cost:.2f}{RESET} | ⏱️ {mins}m {secs}s") 591 ``` 592 593 ```javascript Node.js theme={null} 594 #!/usr/bin/env node 595 const { execSync } = require('child_process'); 596 const path = require('path'); 597 598 let input = ''; 599 process.stdin.on('data', chunk => input += chunk); 600 process.stdin.on('end', () => { 601 const data = JSON.parse(input); 602 const model = data.model.display_name; 603 const dir = path.basename(data.workspace.current_dir); 604 const cost = data.cost?.total_cost_usd || 0; 605 const pct = Math.floor(data.context_window?.used_percentage || 0); 606 const durationMs = data.cost?.total_duration_ms || 0; 607 608 const CYAN = '\x1b[36m', GREEN = '\x1b[32m', YELLOW = '\x1b[33m', RED = '\x1b[31m', RESET = '\x1b[0m'; 609 610 const barColor = pct >= 90 ? RED : pct >= 70 ? YELLOW : GREEN; 611 const filled = Math.floor(pct / 10); 612 const bar = '█'.repeat(filled) + '░'.repeat(10 - filled); 613 614 const mins = Math.floor(durationMs / 60000); 615 const secs = Math.floor((durationMs % 60000) / 1000); 616 617 let branch = ''; 618 try { 619 branch = execSync('git branch --show-current', { encoding: 'utf8', stdio: ['pipe', 'pipe', 'ignore'] }).trim(); 620 branch = branch ? ` | 🌿 ${branch}` : ''; 621 } catch {} 622 623 console.log(`${CYAN}[${model}]${RESET} 📁 ${dir}${branch}`); 624 console.log(`${barColor}${bar}${RESET} ${pct}% | ${YELLOW}$${cost.toFixed(2)}${RESET} | ⏱️ ${mins}m ${secs}s`); 625 }); 626 ``` 627 628 629 ### Clickable links 630 631 This example creates a clickable link to your GitHub repository. It reads the git remote URL, converts SSH format to HTTPS with `sed`, and wraps the repo name in OSC 8 escape codes. Hold Cmd (macOS) or Ctrl (Windows/Linux) and click to open the link in your browser. 632 633 634 635 636 637 Each script gets the git remote URL, converts SSH format to HTTPS, and wraps the repo name in OSC 8 escape codes. The Bash version uses `printf '%b'` which interprets backslash escapes more reliably than `echo -e` across different shells: 638 639 640 ```bash Bash theme={null} 641 #!/bin/bash 642 input=$(cat) 643 644 MODEL=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name') 645 646 # Convert git SSH URL to HTTPS 647 REMOTE=$(git remote get-url origin 2>/dev/null | sed 's/git@github.com:/https:\/\/github.com\//' | sed 's/\.git$//') 648 649 if [ -n "$REMOTE" ]; then 650 REPO_NAME=$(basename "$REMOTE") 651 # OSC 8 format: \e]8;;URL\a then TEXT then \e]8;;\a 652 # printf %b interprets escape sequences reliably across shells 653 printf '%b' "[$MODEL] 🔗 \e]8;;${REMOTE}\a${REPO_NAME}\e]8;;\a\n" 654 else 655 echo "[$MODEL]" 656 fi 657 ``` 658 659 ```python Python theme={null} 660 #!/usr/bin/env python3 661 import json, sys, subprocess, re, os 662 663 data = json.load(sys.stdin) 664 model = data['model']['display_name'] 665 666 # Get git remote URL 667 try: 668 remote = subprocess.check_output( 669 ['git', 'remote', 'get-url', 'origin'], 670 stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL, text=True 671 ).strip() 672 # Convert SSH to HTTPS format 673 remote = re.sub(r'^git@github\.com:', 'https://github.com/', remote) 674 remote = re.sub(r'\.git$', '', remote) 675 repo_name = os.path.basename(remote) 676 # OSC 8 escape sequences 677 link = f"\033]8;;{remote}\a{repo_name}\033]8;;\a" 678 print(f"[{model}] 🔗 {link}") 679 except: 680 print(f"[{model}]") 681 ``` 682 683 ```javascript Node.js theme={null} 684 #!/usr/bin/env node 685 const { execSync } = require('child_process'); 686 const path = require('path'); 687 688 let input = ''; 689 process.stdin.on('data', chunk => input += chunk); 690 process.stdin.on('end', () => { 691 const data = JSON.parse(input); 692 const model = data.model.display_name; 693 694 try { 695 let remote = execSync('git remote get-url origin', { encoding: 'utf8', stdio: ['pipe', 'pipe', 'ignore'] }).trim(); 696 // Convert SSH to HTTPS format 697 remote = remote.replace(/^git@github\.com:/, 'https://github.com/').replace(/\.git$/, ''); 698 const repoName = path.basename(remote); 699 // OSC 8 escape sequences 700 const link = `\x1b]8;;${remote}\x07${repoName}\x1b]8;;\x07`; 701 console.log(`[${model}] 🔗 ${link}`); 702 } catch { 703 console.log(`[${model}]`); 704 } 705 }); 706 ``` 707 708 709 ### Rate limit usage 710 711 Display Claude.ai subscription rate limit usage in the status line. The `rate_limits` object contains `five_hour` (5-hour rolling window) and `seven_day` (weekly) windows. Each window provides `used_percentage` (0-100) and `resets_at` (Unix epoch seconds when the window resets). 712 713 This field is only present for Claude.ai subscribers (Pro/Max) after the first API response. Each script handles the absent field gracefully: 714 715 716 ```bash Bash theme={null} 717 #!/bin/bash 718 input=$(cat) 719 720 MODEL=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name') 721 # "// empty" produces no output when rate_limits is absent 722 FIVE_H=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.rate_limits.five_hour.used_percentage // empty') 723 WEEK=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.rate_limits.seven_day.used_percentage // empty') 724 725 LIMITS="" 726 [ -n "$FIVE_H" ] && LIMITS="5h: $(printf '%.0f' "$FIVE_H")%" 727 [ -n "$WEEK" ] && LIMITS="${LIMITS:+$LIMITS }7d: $(printf '%.0f' "$WEEK")%" 728 729 [ -n "$LIMITS" ] && echo "[$MODEL] | $LIMITS" || echo "[$MODEL]" 730 ``` 731 732 ```python Python theme={null} 733 #!/usr/bin/env python3 734 import json, sys 735 736 data = json.load(sys.stdin) 737 model = data['model']['display_name'] 738 739 parts = [] 740 rate = data.get('rate_limits', {}) 741 five_h = rate.get('five_hour', {}).get('used_percentage') 742 week = rate.get('seven_day', {}).get('used_percentage') 743 744 if five_h is not None: 745 parts.append(f"5h: {five_h:.0f}%") 746 if week is not None: 747 parts.append(f"7d: {week:.0f}%") 748 749 if parts: 750 print(f"[{model}] | {' '.join(parts)}") 751 else: 752 print(f"[{model}]") 753 ``` 754 755 ```javascript Node.js theme={null} 756 #!/usr/bin/env node 757 let input = ''; 758 process.stdin.on('data', chunk => input += chunk); 759 process.stdin.on('end', () => { 760 const data = JSON.parse(input); 761 const model = data.model.display_name; 762 763 const parts = []; 764 const fiveH = data.rate_limits?.five_hour?.used_percentage; 765 const week = data.rate_limits?.seven_day?.used_percentage; 766 767 if (fiveH != null) parts.push(`5h: ${Math.round(fiveH)}%`); 768 if (week != null) parts.push(`7d: ${Math.round(week)}%`); 769 770 console.log(parts.length ? `[${model}] | ${parts.join(' ')}` : `[${model}]`); 771 }); 772 ``` 773 774 775 ### Cache expensive operations 776 777 Your status line script runs frequently during active sessions. Commands like `git status` or `git diff` can be slow, especially in large repositories. This example caches git information to a temp file and only refreshes it every 5 seconds. 778 779 The cache filename needs to be stable across status line invocations within a session, but unique across sessions so concurrent sessions in different repositories don't read each other's cached git state. Process-based identifiers like `$$`, `os.getpid()`, or `process.pid` change on every invocation and defeat the cache. Use the `session_id` from the JSON input instead: it's stable for the lifetime of a session and unique per session. 780 781 Each script checks if the cache file is missing or older than 5 seconds before running git commands: 782 783 784 ```bash Bash theme={null} 785 #!/bin/bash 786 input=$(cat) 787 788 MODEL=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name') 789 DIR=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.workspace.current_dir') 790 SESSION_ID=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.session_id') 791 792 CACHE_FILE="/tmp/statusline-git-cache-$SESSION_ID" 793 CACHE_MAX_AGE=5 # seconds 794 795 cache_is_stale() { 796 [ ! -f "$CACHE_FILE" ] || \ 797 # stat -f %m is macOS, stat -c %Y is Linux 798 [ $(($(date +%s) - $(stat -f %m "$CACHE_FILE" 2>/dev/null || stat -c %Y "$CACHE_FILE" 2>/dev/null || echo 0))) -gt $CACHE_MAX_AGE ] 799 } 800 801 if cache_is_stale; then 802 if git rev-parse --git-dir > /dev/null 2>&1; then 803 BRANCH=$(git branch --show-current 2>/dev/null) 804 STAGED=$(git diff --cached --numstat 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ') 805 MODIFIED=$(git diff --numstat 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ') 806 echo "$BRANCH|$STAGED|$MODIFIED" > "$CACHE_FILE" 807 else 808 echo "||" > "$CACHE_FILE" 809 fi 810 fi 811 812 IFS='|' read -r BRANCH STAGED MODIFIED < "$CACHE_FILE" 813 814 if [ -n "$BRANCH" ]; then 815 echo "[$MODEL] 📁 ${DIR##*/} | 🌿 $BRANCH +$STAGED ~$MODIFIED" 816 else 817 echo "[$MODEL] 📁 ${DIR##*/}" 818 fi 819 ``` 820 821 ```python Python theme={null} 822 #!/usr/bin/env python3 823 import json, sys, subprocess, os, time 824 825 data = json.load(sys.stdin) 826 model = data['model']['display_name'] 827 directory = os.path.basename(data['workspace']['current_dir']) 828 session_id = data['session_id'] 829 830 CACHE_FILE = f"/tmp/statusline-git-cache-{session_id}" 831 CACHE_MAX_AGE = 5 # seconds 832 833 def cache_is_stale(): 834 if not os.path.exists(CACHE_FILE): 835 return True 836 return time.time() - os.path.getmtime(CACHE_FILE) > CACHE_MAX_AGE 837 838 if cache_is_stale(): 839 try: 840 subprocess.check_output(['git', 'rev-parse', '--git-dir'], stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL) 841 branch = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'branch', '--show-current'], text=True).strip() 842 staged = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'diff', '--cached', '--numstat'], text=True).strip() 843 modified = subprocess.check_output(['git', 'diff', '--numstat'], text=True).strip() 844 staged_count = len(staged.split('\n')) if staged else 0 845 modified_count = len(modified.split('\n')) if modified else 0 846 with open(CACHE_FILE, 'w') as f: 847 f.write(f"{branch}|{staged_count}|{modified_count}") 848 except: 849 with open(CACHE_FILE, 'w') as f: 850 f.write("||") 851 852 with open(CACHE_FILE) as f: 853 branch, staged, modified = f.read().strip().split('|') 854 855 if branch: 856 print(f"[{model}] 📁 {directory} | 🌿 {branch} +{staged} ~{modified}") 857 else: 858 print(f"[{model}] 📁 {directory}") 859 ``` 860 861 ```javascript Node.js theme={null} 862 #!/usr/bin/env node 863 const { execSync } = require('child_process'); 864 const fs = require('fs'); 865 const path = require('path'); 866 867 let input = ''; 868 process.stdin.on('data', chunk => input += chunk); 869 process.stdin.on('end', () => { 870 const data = JSON.parse(input); 871 const model = data.model.display_name; 872 const dir = path.basename(data.workspace.current_dir); 873 const sessionId = data.session_id; 874 875 const CACHE_FILE = `/tmp/statusline-git-cache-${sessionId}`; 876 const CACHE_MAX_AGE = 5; // seconds 877 878 const cacheIsStale = () => { 879 if (!fs.existsSync(CACHE_FILE)) return true; 880 return (Date.now() / 1000) - fs.statSync(CACHE_FILE).mtimeMs / 1000 > CACHE_MAX_AGE; 881 }; 882 883 if (cacheIsStale()) { 884 try { 885 execSync('git rev-parse --git-dir', { stdio: 'ignore' }); 886 const branch = execSync('git branch --show-current', { encoding: 'utf8' }).trim(); 887 const staged = execSync('git diff --cached --numstat', { encoding: 'utf8' }).trim().split('\n').filter(Boolean).length; 888 const modified = execSync('git diff --numstat', { encoding: 'utf8' }).trim().split('\n').filter(Boolean).length; 889 fs.writeFileSync(CACHE_FILE, `${branch}|${staged}|${modified}`); 890 } catch { 891 fs.writeFileSync(CACHE_FILE, '||'); 892 } 893 } 894 895 const [branch, staged, modified] = fs.readFileSync(CACHE_FILE, 'utf8').trim().split('|'); 896 897 if (branch) { 898 console.log(`[${model}] 📁 ${dir} | 🌿 ${branch} +${staged} ~${modified}`); 899 } else { 900 console.log(`[${model}] 📁 ${dir}`); 901 } 902 }); 903 ``` 904 905 906 ### Windows configuration 907 908 On Windows, Claude Code runs status line commands through Git Bash. You can invoke PowerShell from that shell: 909 910 911 ```json settings.json theme={null} 912 { 913 "statusLine": { 914 "type": "command", 915 "command": "powershell -NoProfile -File C:/Users/username/.claude/statusline.ps1" 916 } 917 } 918 ``` 919 920 ```powershell statusline.ps1 theme={null} 921 $input_json = $input | Out-String | ConvertFrom-Json 922 $cwd = $input_json.cwd 923 $model = $input_json.model.display_name 924 $used = $input_json.context_window.used_percentage 925 $dirname = Split-Path $cwd -Leaf 926 927 if ($used) { 928 Write-Host "$dirname [$model] ctx: $used%" 929 } else { 930 Write-Host "$dirname [$model]" 931 } 932 ``` 933 934 935 Or run a Bash script directly: 936 937 938 ```json settings.json theme={null} 939 { 940 "statusLine": { 941 "type": "command", 942 "command": "~/.claude/statusline.sh" 943 } 944 } 945 ``` 946 947 ```bash statusline.sh theme={null} 948 #!/usr/bin/env bash 949 input=$(cat) 950 cwd=$(echo "$input" | grep -o '"cwd":"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4) 951 model=$(echo "$input" | grep -o '"display_name":"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4) 952 dirname="${cwd##*[/\\]}" 953 echo "$dirname [$model]" 954 ``` 955 956 957 ## Subagent status lines 958 959 The `subagentStatusLine` setting renders a custom row body for each [subagent](/en/sub-agents) shown in the agent panel below the prompt. Use it to replace the default `name · description · token count` row with your own formatting. 960 961 ```json theme={null} 962 { 963 "subagentStatusLine": { 964 "type": "command", 965 "command": "~/.claude/subagent-statusline.sh" 966 } 967 } 968 ``` 969 970 The command runs once per refresh tick with all visible subagent rows passed as a single JSON object on stdin. The input includes the [base hook fields](/en/hooks#common-input-fields) plus `columns` (the usable row width) and a `tasks` array, where each task has `id`, `name`, `type`, `status`, `description`, `label`, `startTime`, `tokenCount`, `tokenSamples`, and `cwd`. 971 972 Write one JSON line to stdout per row you want to override, in the form `{"id": "", "content": ""}`. The `content` string is rendered as-is, including ANSI colors and OSC 8 hyperlinks. Omit a task's `id` to keep the default rendering for that row; emit an empty `content` string to hide it. 973 974 The same trust and `disableAllHooks` gates that apply to `statusLine` apply here. Plugins can ship a default `subagentStatusLine` in their [`settings.json`](/en/plugins-reference#standard-plugin-layout). 975 976 ## Tips 977 978 * **Test with mock input**: `echo '{"model":{"display_name":"Opus"},"workspace":{"current_dir":"/home/user/project"},"context_window":{"used_percentage":25},"session_id":"test-session-abc"}' | ./statusline.sh` 979 * **Keep output short**: the status bar has limited width, so long output may get truncated or wrap awkwardly 980 * **Cache slow operations**: your script runs frequently during active sessions, so commands like `git status` can cause lag. See the [caching example](#cache-expensive-operations) for how to handle this. 981 982 Community projects like [ccstatusline](https://github.com/sirmalloc/ccstatusline) and [starship-claude](https://github.com/martinemde/starship-claude) provide pre-built configurations with themes and additional features. 983 984 ## Troubleshooting 985 986 **Status line not appearing** 987 988 * Verify your script is executable: `chmod +x ~/.claude/statusline.sh` 989 * Check that your script outputs to stdout, not stderr 990 * Run your script manually to verify it produces output 991 * If `disableAllHooks` is set to `true` in your settings, the status line is also disabled. Remove this setting or set it to `false` to re-enable. 992 * Run `claude --debug` to log the exit code and stderr from the first status line invocation in a session 993 * Ask Claude to read your settings file and execute the `statusLine` command directly to surface errors 994 995 **Status line shows `--` or empty values** 996 997 * Fields may be `null` before the first API response completes 998 * Handle null values in your script with fallbacks such as `// 0` in jq 999 * Restart Claude Code if values remain empty after multiple messages 1000 1001 **Context percentage shows unexpected values** 1002 1003 * Use `used_percentage` for accurate context state rather than cumulative totals 1004 * The `total_input_tokens` and `total_output_tokens` are cumulative across the session and may exceed the context window size 1005 * Context percentage may differ from `/context` output due to when each is calculated 1006 1007 **OSC 8 links not clickable** 1008 1009 * Verify your terminal supports OSC 8 hyperlinks (iTerm2, Kitty, WezTerm) 1010 1011 * Terminal.app does not support clickable links 1012 1013 * If link text appears but isn't clickable, Claude Code may not have detected hyperlink support in your terminal. This commonly affects Windows Terminal and other emulators not in the auto-detection list. Set the `FORCE_HYPERLINK` environment variable to override detection before launching Claude Code: 1014 1015 ```bash theme={null} 1016 FORCE_HYPERLINK=1 claude 1017 ``` 1018 1019 In PowerShell, set the variable in the current session first: 1020 1021 ```powershell theme={null} 1022 $env:FORCE_HYPERLINK = "1"; claude 1023 ``` 1024 1025 * SSH and tmux sessions may strip OSC sequences depending on configuration 1026 1027 * If escape sequences appear as literal text like `\e]8;;`, use `printf '%b'` instead of `echo -e` for more reliable escape handling 1028 1029 **Display glitches with escape sequences** 1030 1031 * Complex escape sequences (ANSI colors, OSC 8 links) can occasionally cause garbled output if they overlap with other UI updates 1032 * If you see corrupted text, try simplifying your script to plain text output 1033 * Multi-line status lines with escape codes are more prone to rendering issues than single-line plain text 1034 1035 **Workspace trust required** 1036 1037 * The status line command only runs if you've accepted the workspace trust dialog for the current directory. Because `statusLine` executes a shell command, it requires the same trust acceptance as hooks and other shell-executing settings. 1038 * If trust isn't accepted, you'll see the notification `statusline skipped · restart to fix` instead of your status line output. Restart Claude Code and accept the trust prompt to enable it. 1039 1040 **Script errors or hangs** 1041 1042 * Scripts that exit with non-zero codes or produce no output cause the status line to go blank 1043 * Slow scripts block the status line from updating until they complete. Keep scripts fast to avoid stale output. 1044 * If a new update triggers while a slow script is running, the in-flight script is cancelled 1045 * Test your script independently with mock input before configuring it 1046 1047 **Notifications share the status line row** 1048 1049 * System notifications like MCP server errors and auto-updates display on the right side of the same row as your status line. Transient notifications such as the context-low warning also cycle through this area. 1050 * Enabling verbose mode adds a token counter to this area 1051 * On narrow terminals, these notifications may truncate your status line output 1052

content.text:
**JSON config for settings.json:**

```json
{
  "statusLine": {
    "type": "command",
    "command": "jq -r '\"Context: \\(.context_window.remaining_percentage // 0 | floor)% remaining\"'"
  }
}
```

Or with more detail using a script at `~/.claude/statusline-context.sh`:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
input=$(cat)
REMAINING=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.context_window.remaining_percentage // 0' | cut -d. -f1)
echo "Context: ${REMAINING}% remaining"
```

Then in settings.json:
```json
{
  "statusLine": {
    "type": "command",
    "command": "~/.claude/statusline-context.sh"
  }
}
```

**Available JSON fields from Claude Code:**

- `context_window.used_percentage` - Pre-calculated % used (0-100)
- `context_window.remaining_percentage` - Pre-calculated % remaining (0-100)
- `context_window.context_window_size` - Total tokens (200000 default)
- `context_window.current_usage` - Object with `input_tokens`, `cache_creation_input_tokens`, `cache_read_input_tokens`

**To compute context % manually:** Add `input_tokens + cache_creation_input_tokens + cache_read_input_tokens`, divide by `context_window_size`, multiply by 100. Claude provides both pre-calculated (`remaining_percentage`) which is simpler, and current usage breakdown if you need per-category breakdown.

Use `remaining_percentage` for remaining % since it's already computed and handles null values gracefully with the `// 0` fallback.
